theory.intro: A recurring decimal repeats the same digit or block of digits forever. Instead of writing dots ($...$), mark the repeat with a dot over a single repeating digit or a bar over a repeating block.
facts: A **single** repeating digit takes a dot above it: $0.6666... = 0.\dot{6}$.
A repeating **block** of two or more digits takes a bar (vinculum) over the block: $0.686868... = 0.\overline{68}$.
Write any non-repeating digits at the front normally, then mark only the part that repeats: $0.5777... = 0.5\dot{7}$.

practice.masteryOmitted: Writing a repeating decimal in dot or bar notation is a single-step transformation with no genuine in-skill twist; any harder task (converting a fraction, then classifying) belongs to neighbouring skills, so a mastery tier would only re-run the same notation step.
